ontology.foodex2_description: Mixed rice dish with vegetables and protein
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated chicken-bibimbap code; A0FKP (mixed rice dish) used as closest defensible analogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for bibimbap; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of bibimbap with chicken is approximately 350 g, representing a typical restaurant or hawker bowl containing rice, mixed vegetables, sliced chicken, egg, and gochujang sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Bibimbap (chicken) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for Bibimbap (chicken), record F05100006. All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice-based mixed dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken bibimbap. GI of 56 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice-based mixed dishes with vegetables and protein (GI range 54–61 for comparable preparations). Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (56 × 44.1) / 100 ≈ 24.7, rounded to 24.8 for a 350 g serving (12.61 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 3.5 = 44.1 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 161 mg per 100 g; moderate potassium contribution from mixed vegetables
hypertension.guidance: At 161 mg sodium per 100 g, a standard 350 g serving delivers approximately 564 mg sodium — within acceptable limits for hypertension management — however patients should request reduced gochujang sauce, as restaurant preparations may substantially increase sodium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice base with estimated GI 56 and low dietary fibre at 0.91 g per 100 g; carbohydrate-to-fibre ratio unfavourable for glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: The white rice base and low fibre content (0.91 g/100 g) produce a moderate glycaemic load (~24.8 per serving); patients with type 2 diabetes should request a brown rice or mixed-grain substitution and limit gochujang quantity to reduce added sugar intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~0.5 g/100 g), no trans fat, mixed vegetables providing antioxidants and fibre
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish is low in saturated fat and contains cardioprotective components including sesame oil (unsaturated fatty acids), mixed vegetables (antioxidants), and lean chicken protein; sodium should be monitored in restaurant portions where gochujang use may be liberal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory vegetable components, sesame oil MUFA/PUFA; gochujang contains nightshade chilli
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The dish provides anti-inflammatory benefit from mixed vegetables, sesame oil, and mushroom beta-glucans; individuals with nightshade sensitivity should reduce or substitute gochujang, and high-sodium sauce use should be minimised to avoid exacerbating tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bibimbap with Chicken (닭고기 비빔밥) is a Korean mixed rice dish consisting of steamed white rice topped with sliced cooked chicken, sautéed mushrooms, julienned carrots, blanched beansprouts, wilted spinach, a fried or soft-boiled egg, and gochujang (Korean fermented chilli soya bean paste), finished with sesame seeds and sesame oil. The dish is assembled in a bowl and mixed thoroughly before eating, producing a harmonious combination of savoury, mildly spicy, nutty, and umami flavours with varied textures — soft rice, tender chicken, crisp vegetables, and a rich egg yolk. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides 100 kcal, 7.47 g protein, 2.09 g fat, and 12.61 g carbohydrate, with 161 mg sodium and 0.91 g dietary fibre. The dish is a moderate glycaemic-load food (estimated GI 56, GL ~24.8 per 350 g serving) due to its white rice base and low fibre content. It is suitable for hypertension management at standard sodium levels but requires portion and sauce control for type 2 diabetes. The dish is halal-certified in Singapore hawker and restaurant contexts and is widely available across Korean restaurants and food courts in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chicken bibimbap?
Korean rice bowl with chicken calories

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore database entry F05100006 at confidence 0.95. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous white rice mixed-dish entries; estimation basis documented. All mandatory multilingual fields populated. Ontology anchors are defensible.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB source): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These detailed fatty acid, micronutrient, and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this dish as of 2026. Saturated fat estimated at 0.50 g/100 g based on total fat 2.09 g and typical saturated fat fraction for lean chicken and vegetable mixed dishes per USDA FoodData Central analogous entries. Input dietary_tags included 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based' which are incorrect for a chicken-containing dish; these have been removed. Input GI value of 0 with null gi_level was unsupported and has been replaced with an analogous estimate of GI 56 from the University of Sydney GI Database. Korean mandatory language coverage added per G6 rules. Singaporean multilingual coverage (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) added as this dish is widely served in Singapore food contexts.
